trace: Did it actually query Shopify/BigQuery, or just describe what it would do?
Are the SKUs, orders, prices and numbers genuine and findable?
Did it answer the exact question you asked?
Read-only where it should be? No customer PII pasted in? No Reservoir write?
Would you publish it / send it / make the call on it as-is?

claude.use: Do this task here. Claude is connected to your real Shopify, BigQuery and Notion — so it works on actual orders and data, drafts in your voice, and you can validate what it really did.

codex.use: Reach for Codex when this turns into something to build or automate — a script to pull a report on a schedule, a small tool, or repeatable code. It's the coding agent, not a data-drafter.

claude.use: Draft and ground the synonyms list here — Claude reads your real Shopify range so the groups tie to real SKUs.

codex.use: If you want the list auto-generated from a product export every month, Codex can write a small script that does it repeatably.

claude.use: Pull and validate the performance figures here — Claude can read the real orders/UTMs behind each number.

codex.use: Codex can build a reusable script that rebuilds this performance summary from a CSV/export each week — same shape every time.

claude.use: Build and sanity-check the model here — Claude keeps each figure traceable to your source cells.

codex.use: For a spreadsheet that regenerates tabs from raw data automatically, Codex can write the formulas/script behind it.

claude.use: Use Claude to actually run the read-only BigQuery queries in plain English and show the team the results.

codex.use: Codex is your build tool — wrap the safe read pattern into a reusable script or tiny internal tool the team can run.

claude.use: Design and test the skill's behaviour here against real data and your knowledge base.

codex.use: Codex implements it — turn the agreed skill spec into actual code/automation the team reuses.

claude.use: Read the Reservoir in plain English here — Claude writes the read-only query and shows it.

codex.use: Any Reservoir change is a build/engineering job — and only Yas writes. Codex work on the data layer goes through Yas.

abbie.title: Find out what marketing data you can actually reach
abbie.real: From your Notion: 'I need to establish exactly what product sales data I have to play with — it shapes the marketing strategy.'

learn.body: Plain GPT talks from memory. Agentic Claude uses connectors to actually look inside Shopify and BigQuery and act on real data. Same confident tone either way — so you must always check what it really did.

steps.p: Get Claude to list the connectors active for you and, in plain English, what each can reach.
Specifically ask what product-sales data is available across Shopify (both stores) and BigQuery.

abbie.prompt: What connectors are active for me, and in plain English what marketing and product-sales data can each one reach across BikeLuggage, MotoPlanet and BigQuery? Show me, don't just tell me.

spot.q: You ask 'how are tank bags selling?' Two answers come back.

good.txt: "I queried Shopify orders for the last 30 days. Tank bags: 42 units across BL+MP. Here are the orders I counted."
good.verdict: Real query, shows its working — checkable.

bad.txt: "Tank bags are selling well, a strong category driven by seasonal touring demand."
bad.verdict: Plausible, but it never looked. A guess dressed as data.

quiz.q: Why can't you trust an answer just because it sounds confident?
opts: Confidence and correctness are unrelated — it may have guessed
quiz.ex: It predicts what an answer looks like. You verify by checking the source, never the tone.

apply.q: In Claude Desktop, run the prompt above, then paste back ONE real data source it told you it can reach (e.g. 'Shopify orders — BikeLuggage'). Seeing it for yourself is the point.
apply.placeholder: Paste one real connector/data source Claude listed for you…

keep.label: data-check prompt
keep.desc: Reuse this any time you're not sure whether Claude is using real data or guessing.
keep.text: Before you answer: which connector and data did you use, and show me the actual records so I can check. If you didn't look anything up, say so.

abbie.title: Build the Searchanise synonyms list — with real SKUs
abbie.real: Straight from your Notion: 'Searchanise > synonyms > provide context of bikes/brands/SKUs to refine (o>0 etc), build a GPT prompt for the task with context source files.'

learn.body: The single biggest lever on output quality is context. Give Claude the real brands, bikes and SKU quirks (like o vs 0) and it produces a usable synonyms list instead of generic guesses.

steps.p: Tell Claude the brands you sell for, common bike-name variations, and SKU quirks (o vs 0, spacing).
Have it draft synonym groups for search, grounded in your actual product range — and explain each group.
Ask which products/terms it based each synonym group on, so you can verify it didn't invent any.

abbie.prompt: Help me build a Searchanise synonyms list for BikeLuggage. Use our real brands and product range (pull from Shopify), include common bike-name variations and SKU quirks like 'o' vs '0'. Group the synonyms, explain each group, and show me the products each group is based on so I can check them.

spot.q: Claude returns 30 synonym groups, beautifully formatted.

good.txt: "Here are 18 groups, each tied to products I found in your catalogue — see the SKU list under each."
good.verdict: Grounded in real products you can check.

bad.txt: "Here are 30 groups covering all common motorcycle luggage search terms."
bad.verdict: No link to your real range — likely padded with generic terms.

quiz.q: Your synonyms list looks great but cites no products. Go or No-Go?
opts: No-Go — no source to check it against
quiz.ex: No traceable source = unverified. Ask it to tie each group to real SKUs before you use it.

apply.q: Run it on ONE real product category you own (tank bags, panniers, tail tidies…). Paste back 2 synonym groups Claude tied to real SKUs — and note any it couldn't source.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'tank bag' group → tied to E09, TR10, TR15… / one group had no SKU so I dropped it

keep.label: Searchanise synonyms prompt
keep.desc: Your grounded synonyms builder — reuse it whenever the search list needs refreshing.
keep.text: Build a Searchanise synonyms list for [category]. Use our real product range (pull from Shopify), include bike-name variations and SKU quirks like 'o' vs '0'. Group them, explain each, and show the SKUs each group is based on so I can verify. Flag any group you can't tie to a real product.

abbie.title: Review newsletter & socials performance — and catch the invented stat
abbie.real: From your Notion: 'Run through newsletters performance · GPT review newsletter + socials performance.'

learn.body: Marketing output looks finished even when a number is wrong. TRACE is your 20-second check: Traceable, Real, Accurate to intent, Contained, Enough to act? Your two sharpest letters are R and A.

steps.p: Get Claude to summarise recent newsletter + social performance from the real data.
Tick each letter only when you've confirmed it. Pay special attention to R (are these numbers real?).

abbie.prompt: Summarise our last 3 newsletters' performance (opens, clicks, revenue) and our recent social performance. Pull the real figures, show me where each number came from, and flag anything you're estimating rather than measuring.

spot.q: The summary says one newsletter drove '£4,200 in attributed revenue'.

good.txt: "…£4,200, from these 17 orders with the campaign UTM — list attached."
good.verdict: Traceable to real orders. Trust it.

bad.txt: "…£4,200, a strong result reflecting good engagement."
bad.verdict: No source for the figure. Run R — could be invented.

quiz.q: Which two TRACE letters matter most for a marketing performance read?
quiz.ex: A figure that isn't Real, or that answers a slightly different question than you asked (Accurate), is the classic marketing trap.

apply.q: Run the performance read on your REAL last newsletter. Find ONE number, then paste back where it came from (the orders/UTM/source) — or write 'couldn't source it' if it won't show you.
apply.placeholder: e.g. '£X revenue → traced to 14 orders with the June UTM' OR 'couldn't source the open-rate figure'

keep.label: performance-with-receipts prompt
keep.desc: Forces every figure to come with its source — your standing marketing-review prompt.
keep.text: Summarise [newsletter/campaign] performance: opens, clicks, revenue. For every number, show me exactly where it came from (orders, UTM, platform export). Flag anything you're estimating rather than measuring — don't fill gaps with plausible figures.

abbie.title: The one rule that protects the whole business
abbie.real: Your BigQuery — the Digital Reservoir — is the single source of truth behind every report.

learn.body: Big jobs get split across specialised agents — a Researcher finds, an Analyst reads data, a Writer drafts, a Reviewer checks — coordinated by an Orchestrator (the 'team lead' agent). You don't build these by hand, but knowing the roles helps you ask for the right help and spot which step went wrong.

steps.h: Reading the Reservoir is safe and encouraged
steps.p: Ask it anything in plain English — Claude writes the query. This is how Marketing and CS get grounded data.

steps.h: Your account CAN write. That is not permission.
steps.p: Capability is not authorisation. A bad write corrupts every report for everyone — not just your work.

steps.n: If it offers to write — STOP
steps.h: That's the exact moment the rule exists for
steps.p: Don't let Claude change, insert, update, delete or 'fix' anything in the Reservoir. Route it to Yas.

abbie.prompt: From our BigQuery data, what were last month's top-selling products on MotoPlanet? Show me the query and the tables you used — read only, do not change anything.

spot.q: Claude finishes a data task and says it can 'tidy up' a table for you in BigQuery.

good.txt: "I can read it and flag what looks off, but I won't change anything — a write to the Reservoir goes through Yas."
good.verdict: Correct — reads, flags, refuses to write.

bad.txt: "Sure — I'll update those rows now to fix them for you."
bad.verdict: This is the breach. Stop and route to Yas.

quiz.q: Your account technically CAN write to the Reservoir. So you may…
opts: Nothing changes: capability isn't authorisation. Read only; writes go to Yas
quiz.ex: Capability is not authorisation. The system allowing it is not the same as being permitted.

quiz.q: An 'orchestrator' agent…
opts: Splits a big task, assigns each piece to the right agent, assembles the result
quiz.ex: Like a pit-crew chief — it coordinates specialists rather than doing it all alone.

apply.q: Run the read-only Reservoir prompt. Paste back the query/tables it showed you — and confirm it did NOT offer to change anything (if it did, that's the lesson).
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Showed query against orders table, read-only, no write offered' OR 'it offered to tidy a table — I declined'

keep.label: Reservoir read rule
keep.desc: The rule that protects every report in the business. Keep it visible.
keep.text: Read the Reservoir freely — ask in plain English, have Claude write the read-only query and show it. NEVER let it write: no change, insert, update, delete or 'fix'. If it offers to write, stop and route to Yas. Capability is not authorisation.

abbie.title: Turn your best prompt into a reusable Skill
abbie.real: You've been collecting 'keep this' prompts all the way through. A Skill is how you stop re-typing them — your team already runs a 'master marketing prompt' and the Alpha Duck CS assistant; those are Skills in the making.

learn.body: A Skill is a saved, reusable instruction set you can run again and again without rewriting it — like a saved recipe for a job you do often. In Claude you save it as a Project/custom instruction; in Codex it's a reusable script or command. Prompt = one-off. Skill = the same job, done consistently, every time.

steps.p: Choose something you do weekly that must come out consistent — a customer reply format, the performance report, a product-copy template.
If it's drafting/validating on your real data → save it as a Claude Project/Skill. If it's code or automation that runs the same way each time → Codex.
A good Skill carries its own validation — 'always show the source/order used' — so it stays trustworthy when reused.

abbie.prompt: Help me turn this prompt into a reusable Skill: [paste one of your 'keep this' prompts]. Tell me whether it belongs in Claude (as a Project/saved instruction) or Codex (as a script), what inputs it needs each time, and what validation check it should always run before I trust its output.

spot.q: You do a one-off press release. Someone says 'let's build a Skill for it.'

good.txt: "It's a one-off — just prompt it. Skills are for jobs we repeat and need consistent."
good.verdict: Right — don't over-build. Prompt the one-offs.

bad.txt: "Yes, let's build a custom Skill and a script for this single release."
bad.verdict: Overkill. A Skill earns its keep only on frequent, consistent work.

quiz.q: When is it worth turning a prompt into a Skill?
opts: When the job is frequent AND must come out consistent
quiz.ex: Frequent + consistency-critical = Skill. A clever one-off is just a prompt.

quiz.q: A 'drafting a customer reply on real order data' Skill belongs in…
opts: Claude — it's connected to your real data and your voice
quiz.ex: Claude is connected to Shopify/Notion and drafts in your voice. Codex is for building/automating, not data-drafting.

quiz.q: What makes a Skill stay trustworthy when reused?
opts: It bakes in its own check — e.g. 'always show the source used'
quiz.ex: A reusable Skill that can't show its working will eventually be trusted blindly. Build the check in.

apply.q: Pick ONE 'keep this' prompt you saved earlier. Write: (a) is it a Claude Skill or a Codex script, and (b) the one check it should always run. Paste it back.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Grounded-reply prompt → Claude Project. Always shows the order it used before I send.'

keep.label: Skill-builder template
keep.desc: Use this to convert any repeated prompt into a proper reusable Skill.
keep.text: Turn this into a reusable Skill: [prompt]. Decide: Claude Project (drafting/validating on real data) or Codex script (build/automate)? Define the inputs it needs each run, and bake in one validation check it must always perform (e.g. 'show the source/order used') before I trust the output.

emyr.title: Map the product data behind your copy
emyr.real: From your Notion: footpeg research, SHAD pannier-kit comparisons, kickstand pads, chain tensioners — all real product copy you produce.

learn.body: GenAI writes fluently from patterns — which means it can invent a feature that sounds right. For branding, fluent is not the same as true, or on-brand. Grounding it in real product data is everything.

steps.p: Ask Claude what product attributes it can pull from Shopify for a given range (e.g. SHAD pannier kits).
Note where the real data is thin — those are exactly the spots where Claude is tempted to invent.

emyr.prompt: For our SHAD pannier fitting kits (SE, SR, 3P, 4P) on MotoPlanet, pull the real product attributes from Shopify — materials, fitment, weight, luggage type. Show me what's actually in the data and where it's missing.

spot.q: You ask for copy on the SHAD 4P kit's frame.

good.txt: "The listing specifies a 20mm steel frame with crossbar — I'll write from that."
good.verdict: Tied to the real spec in your data.

bad.txt: "It features an advanced lightweight alloy frame for maximum durability."
bad.verdict: 'Alloy/lightweight' contradicts your real spec (steel, reinforced). Invented.

quiz.q: GenAI's biggest risk in product copy is…
opts: Inventing a feature that sounds plausible but isn't real
quiz.ex: Every spec must trace to the real product. Fluent ≠ true.

apply.q: Pick ONE product you actually write copy for. Ask Claude for its real attributes from Shopify, then paste back one spec that IS in the data and one gap where it's missing.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'real: 20mm steel frame (4P) / missing: exact weight not in listing'

keep.label: spec-grounding prompt
keep.desc: Stops invented features before they reach your copy.
keep.text: For [product], pull the real attributes from Shopify — materials, fitment, weight, dimensions. Show me what's actually in the data and where it's missing. Do not infer or invent any spec; if it's not in the data, say 'not specified'.

emyr.title: Turn the Voge 900DX research into an on-brand review script
emyr.real: Your Notion has a full Voge 900DX press-pack: positioning, owner praise, fuelling niggles, UK price ladder. Real source material for a real video.

learn.body: Give Claude the angle, the audience, and the must-hit points. With your press-pack as the source, it drafts a script that's grounded — not generic.

steps.p: Point Claude at your Voge 900DX notes as the single source for the script.
'Lot of bike for the money' hook; honest about the low-rpm fuelling; UK rider audience.
Every price and claim must trace to the press-pack — no invented figures.

emyr.prompt: Using my Voge 900DX press-pack notes as the only source, draft a 60-second on-camera intro script. Hook: 'a lot of bike for the money'. Be honest about the low-rpm fuelling. UK rider audience, our brand voice. Every price/claim must come from my notes — flag anything you can't source.

spot.q: The script quotes the Voge at '£8,799 OTR'.

good.txt: "£8,799 OTR — from your press-pack price ladder (checked March 2026)."
good.verdict: Traces to your own source. Good.

bad.txt: "…around £9,500, typical for the class."
bad.verdict: Doesn't match your notes (£8,799). Invented price.

quiz.q: Two checks every branded draft needs are…
opts: R (is it true?) and voice (is it us?)
quiz.ex: Don't let a pass on voice hide a failed fact-check, or vice versa.

apply.q: Take a real bike or product you're reviewing. Draft one opening line from YOUR source notes, then paste it back here — and confirm every claim in it traces to your notes.
apply.placeholder: Paste your one-line hook + 'all claims sourced from my notes: yes/no'

keep.label: review-script prompt
keep.desc: Your repeatable, source-locked script starter for any bike review.
keep.text: Using my press-pack notes as the ONLY source, draft a 60-second on-camera intro for [bike]. Hook: [angle]. Honest about [known niggle]. UK rider audience, our brand voice. Every price/claim must come from my notes — flag anything you can't source rather than inventing it.

emyr.title: Build the revenue-forecast tabs — then validate the model
emyr.real: Direct from your Notion: 'Take this revenue model and create a tab per year, each working in isolation, plus a top-level growth tab. Exclude anything not directly relevant.'

learn.body: With numbers, T and R are everything: does each figure trace to the source model, and is it real — not a plausible interpolation Claude filled in?

steps.p: One tab per year (2026–2029), each self-contained, plus a top-level growth-and-mix tab.
Spot-check that the per-year revenue and channel mix match your real targets (e.g. 2026 → £2m; eBay 30%).

emyr.prompt: Take my revenue forecast model and build a tab per year (2026–2029), each working in isolation, plus a top-level tab showing overall growth and where revenue comes from. Exclude anything not directly relevant. Show me which source cells each tab's figures came from.

spot.q: The 2026 tab shows total revenue of £2.0m and eBay at 30%.

good.txt: "£2.0m and eBay 30% — matches your stated 2026 targets in the source model."
good.verdict: Traces to your real targets.

bad.txt: "£2.3m, interpolated to smooth the growth curve."
bad.verdict: It invented a figure. Your target was £2.0m. No-Go.

quiz.q: On a financial model, the most important TRACE letters are…
quiz.ex: Every number must trace to the source model and be real — not a smoothed guess.

apply.q: On your real revenue model, pick ONE figure Claude produced (e.g. a year's total or a channel %). Paste back which source cell it came from — or 'couldn't trace it'.
apply.placeholder: e.g. '2026 total £2.0m → from the targets row in my source model' OR 'couldn't trace the eBay %'

keep.label: model-build prompt
keep.desc: Builds the tabs AND keeps every figure traceable to your source.
keep.text: Take my revenue model and build a tab per year (2026–2029), each self-contained, plus a top-level growth-and-mix tab. Exclude anything not directly relevant. For every figure, show me which source cell it came from. Do not interpolate or smooth — use only my real numbers.

apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Grounded-reply prompt → Claude Project. Always shows the order it used before I send.'

keep.label: Skill-builder template
keep.desc: Use this to convert any repeated prompt into a proper reusable Skill.
keep.text: Turn this into a reusable Skill: [prompt]. Decide: Claude Project (drafting/validating on real data) or Codex script (build/automate)? Define the inputs it needs each run, and bake in one validation check it must always perform (e.g. 'show the source/order used') before I trust the output.

immy.title: See the customer & order data you can reach — safely
immy.real: From your CS build: the goal is replies grounded in the customer's real order, knowledge base and saved replies — human-approved.

learn.body: Claude can pull the real order through the Shopify connector. The skill is letting it fetch the data — never pasting a customer's personal details into the chat yourself.

steps.p: Ask what customer/order data Claude can pull through Shopify for BL and MP.
Note what counts as personal data (names, addresses, contact) — the connector fetches it; you never paste it.

immy.prompt: What customer and order data can you reach through Shopify for BikeLuggage and MotoPlanet? List what's available, and clearly mark what counts as personal data that should be pulled through the connector rather than pasted into a prompt.

spot.q: A customer emails asking where order #BL13458 is.

good.txt: "Using Shopify, find order #BL13458 and draft a delivery-status reply."
good.verdict: Connector fetches it — you stay hands-off the PII.

bad.txt: You paste the customer's name, address and order details into the chat, then ask for a reply.
bad.verdict: PII now sits in the prompt. Avoid — let the connector do it.

quiz.q: A customer's address should be…
opts: Pulled by the connector, never pasted in
quiz.ex: Keep personal data out of the chat box. The connector is the safe path, every time.

apply.q: Think of a real recent enquiry. Write how you'd ask Claude to pull that order WITHOUT typing the customer's personal details into the chat. Paste your phrasing.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Using Shopify, find order #BL1XXXX and draft a status reply' — no name/address pasted

keep.label: safe-lookup phrasing
keep.desc: Your PII-safe way to get Claude onto a real order every time.
keep.text: Using Shopify, find order #[number] for [BL/MP] and draft a reply about [issue]. Pull the customer and order details through the connector — I won't paste personal data into the chat. Show me the order you used before drafting.

immy.title: Draft a real reply — the tank-bag wind-noise enquiry
immy.real: From your Notion, a real CS scenario: 'Thanks for getting in touch Richard. We understand the discomfort, wind is not fun at higher speeds. Do you have your bike's reg to check stock against?' — plus real tank-bag SKUs (E09, TR10, TR15, TR46).

learn.body: A good reply pulls the real order, applies your knowledge base (warranty: Puig 1yr / SHAD 2yr), and uses your saved-reply voice — then you review and send.

steps.p: Have Claude fetch the actual order and the customer's bike/reg if on file.
Use your saved-reply tone; ask for the reg to check stock; suggest from real available SKUs only.
Confirm every order detail and SKU is real — you approve, then send.

immy.prompt: A customer (Richard) reports wind noise and wants a tank bag for his bike. Pull his order/bike from Shopify if on file, draft a friendly reply in our voice asking for his reg to check stock, and only suggest tank bags we actually have available. Show me the real order and SKUs you used.

spot.q: The draft confidently says 'your TR46 will ship today.'

good.txt: "I found order #BL13573 with a black TR46 — confirming dispatch status before I promise a ship date."
good.verdict: Real order, no over-promise. Good.

bad.txt: "Your TR46 tank bag will be dispatched today!" (no order looked up)
bad.verdict: Invented a fulfilment promise. R fails — could be the wrong customer entirely.

quiz.q: Before sending a reply with order details, you must…
opts: Trust the draft if it reads well
Confirm every order detail is the real order
quiz.ex: Never send order details you haven't confirmed are real — and are the right customer's.

apply.q: Draft a real reply to a genuine recent query using the prompt. Paste back the order number Claude used and confirm you checked it's the RIGHT customer's order before 'sending'.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Used #BL13573, confirmed it's Richard's order, no other customer referenced'

keep.label: grounded-reply prompt
keep.desc: Pulls the real order, applies policy, drafts in your voice — review and send.
keep.text: A customer reports [issue]. Pull their order from Shopify, draft a friendly reply in our voice, apply our policy and warranty rules (Puig 1yr / SHAD 2yr), and only suggest products we actually have. Show me the real order and SKUs you used so I can confirm before sending.

immy.title: Returns to policy — and never leak the wrong customer
immy.real: From your CS knowledge base: returns & labels, the £8.39 credit-request flow, faulty vs change-of-mind shipping, warranty (Puig 1yr / SHAD 2yr).

learn.concept: TRACE for CS — C is king
learn.body: For customer replies, Contained is the make-or-break letter: did it stay to policy, and did it keep this customer's data to this customer? One leaked detail is a serious breach.

steps.p: Have Claude apply the right policy (faulty = we pay; change-of-mind = customer pays).
Check it stayed to policy and exposed no other customer's data or order.

immy.prompt: Draft a returns reply for a customer who received a faulty SHAD top box. Apply our returns policy (faulty vs change-of-mind shipping) and SHAD's 2-year warranty. Use only this customer's order — show me which order — and our knowledge-base wording.

spot.q: The draft helpfully adds: 'Other customers with the same SHAD box also had this issue, like order #BL13469.'

good.txt: "Here's your returns process for order #BL13573 — faulty, so we cover return shipping."
good.verdict: Stays to this customer, to policy. Good.

bad.txt: References another customer's order (#BL13469) in the reply.
bad.verdict: PII leak — another customer's order exposed. C fails. Never send.

quiz.q: For a customer reply, the make-or-break TRACE letter is…
quiz.ex: A reply can read perfectly and still be a breach if it leaks another customer's data or strays from policy.

apply.q: Draft a returns reply for a real faulty-item case. Before 'sending', paste back: which ONE order it references, and confirm no OTHER customer's data appears.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'References only order #BL1XXXX; checked — no other customer mentioned; faulty so we pay return'

keep.label: returns-to-policy prompt
keep.desc: Keeps every returns reply on-policy and contained to one customer.
keep.text: Draft a returns reply for [faulty / change-of-mind]. Apply our returns policy (who pays shipping) and the right warranty (Puig 1yr / SHAD 2yr). Use ONLY this customer's order — show me which one. Never reference another customer's order or data.

immy.title: The one rule that protects the whole business
immy.real: Your BigQuery — the Digital Reservoir — is the single source of truth behind every report.

learn.body: Big jobs get split across specialised agents — a Researcher finds, an Analyst reads data, a Writer drafts, a Reviewer checks — coordinated by an Orchestrator (the 'team lead' agent). You don't build these by hand, but knowing the roles helps you ask for the right help and spot which step went wrong.

steps.h: Reading the Reservoir is safe and encouraged
steps.p: Ask it anything in plain English — Claude writes the query. This is how Marketing and CS get grounded data.

steps.h: Your account CAN write. That is not permission.
steps.p: Capability is not authorisation. A bad write corrupts every report for everyone — not just your work.

steps.n: If it offers to write — STOP
steps.h: That's the exact moment the rule exists for
steps.p: Don't let Claude change, insert, update, delete or 'fix' anything in the Reservoir. Route it to Yas.

immy.prompt: From our BigQuery data, what were last month's top-selling products on MotoPlanet? Show me the query and the tables you used — read only, do not change anything.

spot.q: Claude finishes a data task and says it can 'tidy up' a table for you in BigQuery.

good.txt: "I can read it and flag what looks off, but I won't change anything — a write to the Reservoir goes through Yas."
good.verdict: Correct — reads, flags, refuses to write.

bad.txt: "Sure — I'll update those rows now to fix them for you."
bad.verdict: This is the breach. Stop and route to Yas.

quiz.q: Your account technically CAN write to the Reservoir. So you may…
opts: Nothing changes: capability isn't authorisation. Read only; writes go to Yas
quiz.ex: Capability is not authorisation. The system allowing it is not the same as being permitted.

quiz.q: An 'orchestrator' agent…
opts: Splits a big task, assigns each piece to the right agent, assembles the result
quiz.ex: Like a pit-crew chief — it coordinates specialists rather than doing it all alone.

apply.q: Run the read-only Reservoir prompt. Paste back the query/tables it showed you — and confirm it did NOT offer to change anything (if it did, that's the lesson).
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Showed query against orders table, read-only, no write offered' OR 'it offered to tidy a table — I declined'

keep.label: Reservoir read rule
keep.desc: The rule that protects every report in the business. Keep it visible.
keep.text: Read the Reservoir freely — ask in plain English, have Claude write the read-only query and show it. NEVER let it write: no change, insert, update, delete or 'fix'. If it offers to write, stop and route to Yas. Capability is not authorisation.

immy.title: Turn your best prompt into a reusable Skill
immy.real: You've been collecting 'keep this' prompts all the way through. A Skill is how you stop re-typing them — your team already runs a 'master marketing prompt' and the Alpha Duck CS assistant; those are Skills in the making.

learn.body: A Skill is a saved, reusable instruction set you can run again and again without rewriting it — like a saved recipe for a job you do often. In Claude you save it as a Project/custom instruction; in Codex it's a reusable script or command. Prompt = one-off. Skill = the same job, done consistently, every time.

steps.p: Choose something you do weekly that must come out consistent — a customer reply format, the performance report, a product-copy template.
If it's drafting/validating on your real data → save it as a Claude Project/Skill. If it's code or automation that runs the same way each time → Codex.
A good Skill carries its own validation — 'always show the source/order used' — so it stays trustworthy when reused.

immy.prompt: Help me turn this prompt into a reusable Skill: [paste one of your 'keep this' prompts]. Tell me whether it belongs in Claude (as a Project/saved instruction) or Codex (as a script), what inputs it needs each time, and what validation check it should always run before I trust its output.

spot.q: You do a one-off press release. Someone says 'let's build a Skill for it.'

good.txt: "It's a one-off — just prompt it. Skills are for jobs we repeat and need consistent."
good.verdict: Right — don't over-build. Prompt the one-offs.

bad.txt: "Yes, let's build a custom Skill and a script for this single release."
bad.verdict: Overkill. A Skill earns its keep only on frequent, consistent work.

quiz.q: When is it worth turning a prompt into a Skill?
opts: When the job is frequent AND must come out consistent
quiz.ex: Frequent + consistency-critical = Skill. A clever one-off is just a prompt.

quiz.q: A 'drafting a customer reply on real order data' Skill belongs in…
opts: Claude — it's connected to your real data and your voice
quiz.ex: Claude is connected to Shopify/Notion and drafts in your voice. Codex is for building/automating, not data-drafting.

quiz.q: What makes a Skill stay trustworthy when reused?
opts: It bakes in its own check — e.g. 'always show the source used'
quiz.ex: A reusable Skill that can't show its working will eventually be trusted blindly. Build the check in.

apply.q: Pick ONE 'keep this' prompt you saved earlier. Write: (a) is it a Claude Skill or a Codex script, and (b) the one check it should always run. Paste it back.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Grounded-reply prompt → Claude Project. Always shows the order it used before I send.'

keep.label: Skill-builder template
keep.desc: Use this to convert any repeated prompt into a proper reusable Skill.
keep.text: Turn this into a reusable Skill: [prompt]. Decide: Claude Project (drafting/validating on real data) or Codex script (build/automate)? Define the inputs it needs each run, and bake in one validation check it must always perform (e.g. 'show the source/order used') before I trust the output.

callum.title: See where each person's risks differ
callum.real: As founder you judge output quality across Marketing, Branding and CS — each fails in different ways.

learn.body: The same tool carries different risks per role: Marketing invents stats, Branding invents specs, CS leaks data or over-promises. Good oversight means knowing each failure mode.

steps.p: Do the data-visibility exercise yourself so you know what each person can reach.
Note: Abbie → invented stats; Emyr → invented specs; Immy → PII/over-promise.

callum.prompt: Across BikeLuggage and MotoPlanet, show me what data each connector exposes, and help me list the most likely way an agentic output could go wrong for a Marketing task, a Branding task, and a Customer Service task.

spot.q: You're reviewing a teammate's AI-assisted output.

good.txt: You ask: 'show me where this number/spec/order came from' before approving.
good.verdict: Source-first review. The habit you want to model.

bad.txt: It reads well and sounds confident, so you approve it.
bad.verdict: Tone isn't evidence. Confident-and-wrong is the whole risk.

quiz.q: Good oversight means judging an output by…
opts: How confident and polished it reads
Whether its source can be shown and checked
quiz.ex: Model 'show me the source' — it's the behaviour that makes the whole team safe.

apply.q: Review one real piece of AI-assisted work from your team (or your own). Paste back the ONE question you asked to verify it — 'show me where this came from' style.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Asked Emyr to show the source listing for the frame spec before approving'

keep.label: source-first review line
keep.desc: The one habit that keeps the whole team safe — reuse on every approval.
keep.text: Before I approve this: show me where each number / spec / order detail came from, and confirm it was read from real data rather than generated. If you can't show a source, flag it.

callum.title: Set the team's go/no-go and escalation norms
callum.real: You own targets, cost approvals (e.g. Klaviyo), and delegation — you set what 'safe to act on' means here.

learn.body: After TRACE, the call is simple: GO (source shown, checks out, stayed safe) or NO-GO (can't show source, a figure won't reconcile, strayed off-limits → fix or escalate to Yas).

steps.p: Source shown and spot-checked; answers the real question; stayed read-only; no PII exposed.
Anything needing a Reservoir write, or anything unsure, goes to Yas — make asking feel easy.

callum.prompt: Help me write a one-paragraph 'go / no-go' rule for the team: when an AI-assisted output is safe to act on, and when it must be checked or escalated to Yas. Keep it plain and memorable.

spot.q: A teammate's report has one figure that won't reconcile.

good.txt: "No-Go until that figure traces — everything else can wait on it."
good.verdict: One broken number = pause. Right call.

bad.txt: "The rest looks fine, ship it."
bad.verdict: One unreconciled figure can invalidate the conclusion. No-Go.

quiz.q: 'It can't show me where the number came from.' That's a…
quiz.ex: No visible source = No-Go. Don't act until it's traced.

apply.q: Write your team's go/no-go rule in your OWN words (one or two sentences). Paste it here — this becomes the norm you actually share with everyone.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'GO if the source is shown and checks out and nothing customer-facing leaked. Otherwise No-Go → Yas.'

keep.label: go/no-go rule
keep.desc: Your team's shared standard for when AI output is safe to act on.
keep.text: GO when: the source is shown and spot-checked, it answers the real question, it stayed read-only, and no customer data was exposed. NO-GO when: it can't show a source, a figure won't reconcile, or it strayed off-limits — fix it or escalate to Yas. Capability is not authorisation.

callum.real: Your BigQuery — the Digital Reservoir — is the single source of truth behind every report.

keep.label: Skill-builder template
keep.desc: Use this to convert any repeated prompt into a proper reusable Skill.
keep.text: Turn this into a reusable Skill: [prompt]. Decide: Claude Project (drafting/validating on real data) or Codex script (build/automate)? Define the inputs it needs each run, and bake in one validation check it must always perform (e.g. 'show the source/order used') before I trust the output.

yas.title: Be the validation coach, not the rescuer
yas.real: You're the only Reservoir writer and the most agentic-experienced — your job is lifting everyone's judgment.

learn.body: The win isn't you fixing outputs fast — it's the team learning to run TRACE themselves. Surface real failures live (a confident-wrong answer, an invented order, a write offer) as teaching moments.

steps.p: Confirm what each person can actually reach, and the read/write reality of the Reservoir per account.
Document a plain-English BigQuery read pattern others can reuse, with 'show the query' baked in.

yas.prompt: Document a safe, reusable BigQuery read pattern for the non-technical team: plain-English question in, Claude writes the read-only query, always shows the query + tables, and never writes. Include a worked example against our product-sales data.

spot.q: Abbie's stuck on a data task during a session.

good.txt: You ask 'what does TRACE say here?' and let her find the gap.
good.verdict: Coaches the judgment. That's the goal.

bad.txt: You take her keyboard and fix it in 20 seconds.
bad.verdict: Fast, but her judgment didn't improve. Resist rescuing.

quiz.q: Your highest-value move as coach is…
opts: Getting people to run TRACE themselves
quiz.ex: Their judgment improving is the win — not your speed.

apply.q: Document the safe read pattern for one real query the team needs. Paste back the plain-English question + confirm it's read-only and 'shows the query'.
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Q: top sellers last month → read-only, returns query + tables shown, no writes'

keep.label: safe read pattern
keep.desc: The reusable, copy-paste pattern you hand the non-technical team.
keep.text: [Plain-English question] — read only. Write the BigQuery query for me, run it read-only, and always show me the query and the tables you used. Never modify, insert, update or delete anything in the Reservoir.

yas.title: Build one reusable skill the team relies on
yas.real: Abbie already maintains a 'master marketing prompt' and a GPT project; you can turn the best repeated jobs into proper skills.

learn.body: Prompt for one-offs. Build a skill when a task is frequent AND must come out consistent — brand voice, a standard CS reply format, a weekly report shape.

steps.p: E.g. the validated-customer-reply format, or the marketing performance read.

steps.h: Package it with the check built in
steps.p: Bake 'show your working' and the relevant TRACE letters into the skill itself.

yas.prompt: Help me design a reusable 'validated customer reply' skill for the CS team: it pulls the real order, applies our knowledge base and warranty rules, drafts in our voice, and always shows the order it used so the agent can confirm before sending.

spot.q: Someone wants to build a skill for a one-off press release.

good.txt: "That's a one-off — just prompt it. Skills are for frequent + consistent jobs."
good.verdict: Right call — don't over-build.

bad.txt: "Sure, let's build a custom skill for it."
bad.verdict: Overkill for a one-off. A prompt is enough.

quiz.q: Build a custom skill when a task is…
opts: Frequent AND needs to be consistent
quiz.ex: Frequent + consistency-critical = skill. One-offs just get a prompt.

apply.q: Pick one frequent+consistent team task. Paste back: what the skill does, and the ONE check you'd bake into it (e.g. 'always shows the order used').
apply.placeholder: e.g. 'Validated CS reply skill → always shows the order + applies warranty rules before drafting'

keep.label: skill spec starter
keep.desc: A template for specifying any reusable skill with validation built in.
keep.text: Design a reusable skill for [frequent task]. It should: pull the real data ([source]), apply our rules ([policy/KB]), produce [output] in our voice, and ALWAYS show the source it used so a human can verify before acting. Bake the check in — don't make it optional.
